54. Thou shalt not take thy brother's garment; thou shalt pay for
 that which thou shalt receive of thy brother;
ox or his sheep go astray, and hide thyself from them; thou shalt in any case bring them again uxto thy brother.

Both the priest and the Levite in Christ’s parable should have remembered the requirements of the law: “Thou shalt not see thy brother’s ass or his ox fall down by the way, and hide thyself from them: thou shalt surely help him to lift them up again” (Deuteronomy 22:4). And if an ox, how much more should one be willing to help a brother in need. But as Elder James E. Talmage wrote, “Excuses [not…

The rabbinical law allowed for better treatment of animals than it did for humans—particularly on the Sabbath day. A man was exempted from the strictness of the law when extracting an ox out of the mire or feeding and watering his oxen, but to help another Israelite was forbidden if it required even the appearance of work. dreadful bondage, leaving none behind; we have willingly sold and otherwise left our possessions, at the same time leaving friends, parents, companions, &c., behind. The distance to their land of promise was but a few miles from the country of their bondage, while a great many of this people have traversed over one-half of the globe to reach the valleys of Utah. Brother Goddard spoke this morning…

And lest Esau should think that he had achieved nothing of consequence, he added: “And I have oxen and asses”, these being the symbols of two grades of severity that are never combined together save to bring suffering on the world. (This is the underlying reason of the precept: “Thou shalt not plow with an ox and an ass together” (Deut. 22, 10)).

And lest Esau should think that he had achieved nothing of consequence, he added: “And I have oxen and asses”, these being the symbols of two grades of severity that are never combined together save to bring suffering on the world. (This is the underlying reason of the precept: “Thou shalt not plow with an ox and an ass together” (Deut. 22, 10)).

And lest Esau should think that he had achieved nothing of consequence, he added: “And I have oxen and asses”, these being the symbols of two grades of severity that are never combined together save to bring suffering on the world. (This is the underlying reason of the precept: “Thou shalt not plow with an ox and an ass together” (Deut. 22, 10)).

44 If any man take a wife, and go in unto her, and hate her, and give occasions of speech against her, and bring up an evil name upon her, and say, I took this woman, and when I came to her, I found her not a maid.” And, 44 if this thing be true, and the tokens of virginity be not found for the damsel : then they shall bring oat Ibe damsel to the door of her father’s house, and the men of her…
